# [Biology for Majors I](https://courses.lumenlearning.com/wm-biology1/ "Biology for Majors I")

### Module 7: Cell Communication

## Types of Signals

### Learning Outcomes

There are four categories of chemical signaling found in multicellular organisms: paracrine signaling, endocrine signaling, autocrine signaling, and direct signaling across gap junctions (Figure 1). The main difference between the different categories of signaling is the distance that the signal travels through the organism to reach the target cell. Not all cells are affected by the same signals.

![The illustration shows four forms of chemical signaling. In autocrine signaling, a cell targets itself. In signaling across a gap junction, a cell targets a cell connected via gap junctions. In paracrine signaling, a cell targets a nearby cell. In endocrine signaling, a cell targets a distant cell via the bloodstream](https://openstax.org/resources/a38d3e4b11d54233a89abf33a4eb4ac3dc323f51)

Figure 1. In chemical signaling, a cell may target itself (autocrine signaling), a cell connected by gap junctions, a nearby cell (paracrine signaling), or a distant cell (endocrine signaling). Paracrine signaling acts on nearby cells, endocrine signaling uses the circulatory system to transport ligands, and autocrine signaling acts on the signaling cell. Signaling via gap junctions involves signaling molecules moving directly between adjacent cells.

## Paracrine Signaling

![This illustration shows closely juxtaposed bulbous protrusions of presynaptic and postsynaptic cells. The presynaptic cell stores neurotransmitter in synaptic vesicles. When signaling occurs, the vesicles fuse with the cell membrane, thereby releasing the neutrotransmitter, which then binds to receptors on the postsynaptic cell. An enzyme on the surface of the postsynaptic cell destroys the neurotrasmitter, thereby terminating the signal.](https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/courses-images/wp-content/uploads/sites/110/2016/05/02233201/Figure_09_01_02.jpg)

Figure 2. The distance between the presynaptic cell and the postsynaptic cell—called the synaptic gap—is very small and allows for rapid diffusion of the neurotransmitter. Enzymes in the synaptic cleft degrade some types of neurotransmitters to terminate the signal.

Signals that act locally between cells that are close together are called **paracrine signals**. Paracrine signals move by diffusion through the extracellular matrix. These types of signals usually elicit quick responses that last only a short amount of time. In order to keep the response localized, paracrine ligand molecules are normally quickly degraded by enzymes or removed by neighboring cells. Removing the signals will reestablish the concentration gradient for the signal, allowing them to quickly diffuse through the intracellular space if released again.

One example of paracrine signaling is the transfer of signals across synapses between nerve cells. A nerve cell consists of a cell body, several short, branched extensions called dendrites that receive stimuli, and a long extension called an axon, which transmits signals to other nerve cells or muscle cells. The junction between nerve cells where signal transmission occurs is called a synapse. A **synaptic signal** is a chemical signal that travels between nerve cells. Signals within the nerve cells are propagated by fast-moving electrical impulses. When these impulses reach the end of the axon, the signal continues on to a dendrite of the next cell by the release of chemical ligands called **neurotransmitters** by the presynaptic cell (the cell emitting the signal). The neurotransmitters are transported across the very small distances between nerve cells, which are called **chemical synapses** (Figure 2). The small distance between nerve cells allows the signal to travel quickly; this enables an immediate response, such as, Take your hand off the stove!

When the neurotransmitter binds the receptor on the surface of the postsynaptic cell, the electrochemical potential of the target cell changes, and the next electrical impulse is launched. The neurotransmitters that are released into the chemical synapse are degraded quickly or get reabsorbed by the presynaptic cell so that the recipient nerve cell can recover quickly and be prepared to respond rapidly to the next synaptic signal.

## Endocrine Signaling

Signals from distant cells are called **endocrine signals**, and they originate from **endocrine cells**. (In the body, many endocrine cells are located in endocrine glands, such as the thyroid gland, the hypothalamus, and the pituitary gland.) These types of signals usually produce a slower response but have a longer-lasting effect. The ligands released in endocrine signaling are called hormones, signaling molecules that are produced in one part of the body but affect other body regions some distance away.

Hormones travel the large distances between endocrine cells and their target cells via the bloodstream, which is a relatively slow way to move throughout the body. Because of their form of transport, hormones get diluted and are present in low concentrations when they act on their target cells. This is different from paracrine signaling, in which local concentrations of ligands can be very high.

## Autocrine Signaling

**Autocrine signals** are produced by signaling cells that can also bind to the ligand that is released. This means the signaling cell and the target cell can be the same or a similar cell (the prefix *auto-* means self, a reminder that the signaling cell sends a signal to itself). This type of signaling often occurs during the early development of an organism to ensure that cells develop into the correct tissues and take on the proper function. Autocrine signaling also regulates pain sensation and inflammatory responses. Further, if a cell is infected with a virus, the cell can signal itself to undergo programmed cell death, killing the virus in the process. In some cases, neighboring cells of the same type are also influenced by the released ligand. In embryological development, this process of stimulating a group of neighboring cells may help to direct the differentiation of identical cells into the same cell type, thus ensuring the proper developmental outcome.

## Direct Signaling Across Gap Junctions

Gap junctions in animals and plasmodesmata in plants are connections between the plasma membranes of neighboring cells. These water-filled channels allow small signaling molecules, called **intracellular mediators**, to diffuse between the two cells. Small molecules, such as calcium ions (Ca2+), are able to move between cells, but large molecules like proteins and DNA cannot fit through the channels. The specificity of the channels ensures that the cells remain independent but can quickly and easily transmit signals. The transfer of signaling molecules communicates the current state of the cell that is directly next to the target cell; this allows a group of cells to coordinate their response to a signal that only one of them may have received. In plants, plasmodesmata are ubiquitous, making the entire plant into a giant communication network.

## The Cell: A Molecular Approach. 2nd edition.

# Summary

## SIGNALING MOLECULES AND THEIR RECEPTORS

### Modes of Cell-Cell Signaling

Most signaling molecules are secreted by one cell and bind to receptors expressed by a target cell. Cell-cell signaling is divided into three general categories (endocrine, paracrine, and [autocrine signaling](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2925/)) based on the distance over which signals are transmitted.

**KEY TERMS:**
[endocrine signaling](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3035), hormone, [paracrine signaling](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3229), [autocrine signaling](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A2925)

### Steroid Hormones and the Steroid Receptor Superfamily

The [steroid hormones](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3367/), [thyroid hormone](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3387/), vitamin D3, and retinoic acid are small [hydrophobic](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3122/) molecules that diffuse across the [plasma membrane](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3256/) of their target cells and bind to intracellular receptors. Members of the [steroid receptor superfamily](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3368/) function as [transcription](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3391/) factors to directly regulate [gene](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3080/) expression in response to [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/) binding.

**KEY TERMS:** steroid hormone, [testosterone](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3384), [estrogen](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3048), [progesterone](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3279), corticosteroid, glucocorticoid, mineralocorticoid, [ecdysone](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3028), [thyroid hormone](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3387), vitamin D3, retinoic acid, retinoid, [steroid receptor superfamily](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3368)

### Nitric Oxide and Carbon Monoxide

The simple gases nitric oxide and carbon monoxide are important [paracrine signaling](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3229/) molecules in the nervous system and other cell types.

### Neurotransmitters

Neurotransmitters are small [hydrophilic](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3121/) molecules that carry signals between neurons or between neurons and other target cells at a [synapse](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3371/). Many neurotransmitters bind to [ligand](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3154/)-gated ion channels.

**KEY TERMS:**
[neurotransmitter](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3198)

### Peptide Hormones and Growth Factors

The widest variety of signaling molecules in animals are peptides, ranging from only a few to more than a hundred amino acids. This group of molecules includes peptide [hormones](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3120/), neuropeptides, and [growth factors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3104/).

**KEY TERMS:** peptide hormone, neuropeptide, enkephalin, endorphin, neurohormone, growth factor, nerve growth factor (NGF), neurotrophin, [epidermal growth factor](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3044/) (EGF), [platelet-derived growth factor](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3261/) (PDGF), cytokine, membrane-anchored growth factor

### Eicosanoids

The eicosanoids are a class of [lipids](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3157/) that function in paracrine and [autocrine signaling](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2925/).

**KEY TERMS:**
[eicosanoid](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3030), prostaglandin, prostacyclin, thromboxane, leukotriene

### Plant Hormones

Small molecules known as [plant hormones](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3255/) regulate plant growth and development.

**KEY TERMS:** plant hormone, auxin, gibberellin, cytokinin, abscisic acid, ethylene

## FUNCTIONS OF CELL SURFACE RECEPTORS

### G Protein-Coupled Receptors

The largest family of cell surface receptors, including the receptors for many [hormones](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3120/) and neurotransmitters, transmit signals to intracellular targets via the intermediary action of G [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/).

**KEY TERMS:** G protein, [G protein-coupled receptor](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3075/), heterotrimeric G protein

### Receptor Protein-Tyrosine Kinases

The receptors for most [growth factors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3104/) are protein-tyrosine kinases.

**KEY TERMS:**
[receptor protein-tyrosine kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3310), autophosphorylation, [SH2 domain](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3349)

### Cytokine Receptors and Nonreceptor Protein-Tyrosine Kinases

The receptors for many [cytokines](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3005/) act in association with nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinases.

**KEY TERMS:** cytokine receptor superfamily, [nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3202), [Src](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3361), [Janus kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3139/) (JAK)

### Receptors Linked to Other Enzymatic Activities

Other kinds of cell surface receptors include protein-tyrosine phosphatases, protein-serine/ threonine kinases, and guanylyl cyclases.

**KEY TERMS:**
[protein-tyrosine phosphatase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3296), transforming growth factor β (TGF-β), [protein-serine/threonine kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3294), [guanylyl cyclase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3106)

## PATHWAYS OF INTRACELLULAR SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION

### The cAMP Pathway: Second Messengers and Protein Phosphorylation

Cyclic AMP is an important [second messenger](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3344/) in the response of animal cells to a variety of [hormones](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3120/) and odorants. Most actions of cAMP are mediated by [protein kinase A](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3291/), which phosphorylates both metabolic [enzymes](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3042/) and the [transcription factor](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3390/) CREB.

**KEY TERMS:**
[intracellular signal transduction](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3135), [cyclic AMP (cAMP)](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2999/), [second messenger](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3344), [adenylyl cyclase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A2898), [cAMP phosphodiesterase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A2942), [cAMP-dependent protein kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2943/) ([protein kinase A](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3291/)), cAMP response element (CRE), CREB

### Cyclic GMP

Cyclic GMP is also an important [second messenger](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3344/) in animal cells. Its best-characterized role is in visual reception in the vertebrate eye.

**KEY TERMS:** [cyclic GMP (cGMP)](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3001/), [rhodopsin](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3324), [transducin](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3394), [cGMP phosphodiesterase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A2965)

### Phospholipids and Ca2+

Phospholipids and Ca2+ are common second messengers activated downstream of both G protein-coupled receptors and protein-tyrosine kinases. Hydrolysis of phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2) yields [diacylglycerol](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3012/) and inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate (IP3), which activate [protein kinase C](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3292/) and mobilize Ca2+ from intracellular stores, respectively. Increased levels of cytosolic Ca2+ then activate a variety of target [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/), including Ca2+/[calmodulin](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2940/)-dependent protein kinases. In electrically excitable cells of nerve and muscle, levels of cytosolic Ca2+ are increased by the opening of voltage-gated Ca2+ channels in the [plasma membrane](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3256/) and [ryanodine receptors](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3336/) in the endoplasmic and [sarcoplasmic reticulum](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3340/). In addition to being cleaved into diacylglycerol and IP3, PIP2 can be phosphorylated to the distinct [second messenger](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3344/) PIP3.

**KEY TERMS:** phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2), [phospholipase C](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3247), [diacylglycerol](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3012), inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate (IP3), [protein kinase C](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3292), phorbol ester, [calmodulin](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A2940), CaM kinase, ryanodine receptor, phosphatidylinositide (PI) 3-kinase, phosphatidylinositol 3,4,5-trisphosphate (PIP3), [Akt](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A2900)

### Ras, Raf, and the MAP Kinase Pathway

The MAP kinase pathway is a conserved chain of protein kinases activated downstream of a variety of extracellular signals. In animal cells, the best-characterized forms of MAP kinase are coupled to growth factor receptors by the small GTP-binding protein [Ras](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3308/), which initiates a [protein kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3290/) cascade leading to MAP kinase (ERK) activation. ERK then phosphorylates a variety of cytosolic and nuclear [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/), including [transcription](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3391/) factors that mediate immediate-early [gene](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3080/) induction.

**KEY TERMS:** MAP kinase, ERK, [Ras](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3308), [Raf](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3306), MEK, [guanine](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3105/) [nucleotide](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3214/) exchange factor, GTPase-activating protein, small GTP-binding protein, immediate-early [gene](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3080/), serum response element (SRE), serum response factor (SRF), Elk-1

### The JAK/STAT Pathway

[STAT proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3364/) are [transcription](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3391/) factors that contain SH2 [domains](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3021/) and are activated directly by protein-tyrosine kinases associated with cytokine and growth factor receptors.

**KEY TERMS:** [JAK](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3139/)/STAT pathway, STAT protein

## SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION AND THE CYTOSKELETON

### Integrins and Signal Transduction

Binding of integrins to the [extracellular matrix](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3056/) stimulates the [FAK](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3058/) [nonreceptor protein-tyrosine kinase](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3202/), leading to activation of MAP kinase and other downstream signaling pathways.

**KEY TERMS:**
[FAK](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3058)

### Regulation of the Actin Cytoskeleton

Growth factors induce alterations in cell movement and cell shape by remodeling the [actin](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2889/) [cytoskeleton](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3008/). These cytoskeletal alterations are mediated by members of the Rho subfamily of small GTP-binding [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/).

**KEY TERMS:** Rho, Rac, Cdc42

## SIGNALING IN DEVELOPMENT AND DIFFERENTIATION

### The Receptor Tyrosine Kinase/Ras/MAP Kinase Pathway in Drosophila and C. elegans

The role of the [Ras](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3308/)/[Raf](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3306/)/MAP kinase pathway in development has been elucidated by studies of the differentiation of photoreceptor neurons in *Drosophila* and vulval induction in *C. elegans*.

### Hedgehog and Wingless

The Hedgehog and Wingless signaling pathways play key roles in determination of cell fate and patterning during development of both invertebrate and vertebrate embryos.

**KEY TERMS:** Hedgehog, Wingless

### Notch Signaling

The Notch pathway controls cell fate by direct cell-cell interactions during animal development.

**KEY TERMS:** Notch

## REGULATION OF PROGRAMMED CELL DEATH

### Caspases and Apoptosis

Programmed cell death plays a key role both in the maintenance of adult tissues and embryonic development. In contrast to the accidental death of cells from an acute injury, [programmed cell death](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3280/) takes place by the active process of [apoptosis](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2918/). Genes responsible for the regulation and execution of apoptosis are conserved from *C. elegans* to humans. These components of the cell death machinery include a family of proteases ([caspases](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2950/)) that are the effectors of apoptosis as well as [proteins](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3297/) that regulate caspase activation.

**KEY TERMS:**
[programmed cell death](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A3280), [apoptosis](/books/n/cooper/A2886/#A2918), caspase, Bcl-2

### Cell Death Receptors and Caspase Activation

Some secreted polypeptides induce [programmed cell death](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3280/) by activating receptors that are directly linked to [caspases](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2950/).

**KEY TERMS:** [tumor necrosis factor](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A3408/) (TNF)

### Signaling Cell Survival

Many cells are dependent on survival signals from secreted factors or cell-cell contacts that suppress [apoptosis](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2918/). The PI 3-kinase/[Akt](/books/n/cooper/A2886/def-item/A2900/) pathway is a major signaling pathway responsible for promoting cell survival.

##### Learning Objectives

*   Describe four types of signaling found in multicellular organisms

Forms of Signaling
------------------

There are four categories of chemical signaling found in multicellular organisms: paracrine signaling, endocrine signaling, autocrine signaling, and direct signaling across gap junctions. The main difference between the different categories of signaling is the distance that the signal travels through the organism to reach the target cell. It is also important to note that not all cells are affected by the same signals.

![Image 9: image](https://bio.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/78157/figure-09-01-01.jpeg?revision=1)

Figure 9.2.1 9.2.1: Forms of Chemical Signaling: In chemical signaling, a cell may target itself (autocrine signaling), a cell connected by gap junctions, a nearby cell (paracrine signaling), or a distant cell (endocrine signaling). Paracrine signaling acts on nearby cells, endocrine signaling uses the circulatory system to transport ligands, and autocrine signaling acts on the signaling cell. Signaling via gap junctions involves signaling molecules moving directly between adjacent cells.

Paracrine Signaling
-------------------

Signals that act locally between cells that are close together are called paracrine signals. Paracrine signals move by diffusion through the extracellular matrix. These types of signals usually elicit quick responses that last only a short amount of time. In order to keep the response localized, paracrine ligand molecules are normally quickly degraded by enzymes or removed by neighboring cells. Removing the signals will reestablish the concentration gradient for the signal, allowing them to quickly diffuse through the intracellular space if released again.

One example of paracrine signaling is the transfer of signals across synapses between nerve cells. A nerve cell consists of a cell body, several short, branched extensions called dendrites that receive stimuli, and a long extension called an axon, which transmits signals to other nerve cells or muscle cells. The junction between nerve cells where signal transmission occurs is called a synapse. A synaptic signal is a chemical signal that travels between nerve cells. Signals within the nerve cells are propagated by fast-moving electrical impulses. When these impulses reach the end of the axon, the signal continues on to a dendrite of the next cell by the release of chemical ligands called neurotransmitters by the presynaptic cell (the cell emitting the signal). enriched_points.expanded_content: Autocrine signaling occurs when a cell secretes signaling molecules that bind to receptors on itself, enabling self-regulation and influencing its own behavior.
Paracrine signaling involves nearby cells communicating via signaling molecules that diffuse through the extracellular matrix, allowing for localized responses and quick actions.
Endocrine signaling transports hormones through the bloodstream from one part of the body to distant target cells, facilitating long-range communication with sustained effects.
Juxtacrine signaling requires direct contact between neighboring cells, where membrane-bound signaling molecules interact with receptors on adjacent cells to initiate signaling.
